worth_it_explanation: Amazon DSP ships default attribution windows that were designed to be legible, not to fit every product category or campaign type. A display campaign for a considered purchase, such as a mattress or a laptop, may convert weeks after the impression, and a 14-day view-through window misses most of that signal. A fast-moving consumables campaign may attribute to display impressions that had nothing to do with a same-day repeat purchase. Neither problem announces itself in reporting; both inflate or distort apparent return on ad spend (ROAS). The check is a short conversation with the settings and a look at the category purchase cycle.
intro: Amazon DSP attributes conversions to two interaction types: click-through and view-through, each with its own lookback window. Click-through attribution assigns credit when a shopper clicks an ad and then converts within the window. View-through attribution assigns credit when a shopper sees an ad without clicking and then converts within a separate, usually longer window. Amazon's defaults are sensible starting points, but they are not calibrated to every product category or purchase cycle. These checks confirm that the active windows reflect a deliberate decision rather than an untouched default, that the view-through window does not claim credit for conversions display is unlikely to have influenced, and that the attribution model was chosen with awareness of its limits for multi-format campaigns.

audit_checks.title: Attribution lookback windows have not been reviewed against the product purchase cycle
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Mismatched windows inflate reported ROAS for short-cycle products by capturing repurchases that were going to happen anyway, or undercount real influence for long-cycle products by expiring before the shopper decides. Either distortion steers budget optimization toward an inaccurate signal.

educate.base:
Amazon DSP reports conversions under the attribution window that is active on the line item at the time the conversion is counted. The default is a 14-day click-through window and a 14-day view-through window. For many endemic advertisers selling everyday products, 14 days is a reasonable span. For categories with longer consideration cycles, such as electronics, appliances, or furniture, a shopper who saw a display ad in week one may not buy until week three or four. A 14-day view-through window never registers that conversion, and the line item's reported performance looks weaker than it was.

The opposite problem exists for fast-moving consumables, subscription products, or anything a shopper buys on a near-automatic schedule. A 14-day or 30-day view-through window can attribute a repurchase to an impression the shopper barely noticed because the purchase was going to happen regardless. The window's length becomes a claim about causation: 'this impression influenced a conversion that happened within X days.' A window longer than a typical repurchase cycle makes that claim for most conversions in the reporting.

Amazon does allow window customization at the line item level within the bounds the platform sets. The platform also separates 'purchase' and 'detail page view' conversion events, each configurable. A few signs suggest the windows have not been reviewed:
  • All line items carry the same default windows regardless of product category or campaign objective
  • No notes in the campaign record explain why the windows were set to their current values
  • The account team cannot describe the typical purchase cycle for the products in the campaign
You may want to begin with the product category's typical consideration timeline, then choose a click-through window that covers most purchase decisions and a view-through window short enough that it is plausible the impression contributed.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For endemic advertisers selling on Amazon, you may want to consult the Amazon Standard Identification Number (ASIN)'s purchase pattern data in the DSP console or in Amazon Marketing Cloud (AMC). AMC can show the median days-to-purchase from first impression, which gives a data-grounded basis for setting the view-through window rather than estimating from category intuition.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For non-endemic advertisers driving to an off-Amazon destination, the purchase cycle in the advertiser's own analytics, such as days from first visit to conversion, is the reference point for click-through window length. View-through attribution here is harder to validate because the Amazon Ad Tag pixel fires on-site actions rather than on-Amazon purchases, and the connection between a display impression and a later off-Amazon conversion is more attenuated.

learn_more.source_title: Amazon Marketing Cloud: Discover advertising insights
Amazon DSP: Advertise with a demand-side platform

investigate.intro: Confirm the active attribution windows on the account's line items and compare them to the product purchase cycle.

amazon_dsp_console.label: Amazon DSP console
steps: Open the Amazon DSP console and navigate to an active order. Select a display or video line item and open its settings.
Find the attribution settings section and note the click-through window and the view-through window that are currently active.
Repeat for a sample of line items across different campaign objectives, such as prospecting versus retargeting. Note whether the windows are identical across all line items or vary by objective.
Ask the account team or review any internal notes for documentation of why those windows were chosen. If no rationale exists, the windows are likely unchanged defaults.

amazon_marketing_cloud.label: Amazon Marketing Cloud (AMC)
steps: If the account has AMC access, run a path-to-conversion query that shows the median days from first DSP impression to purchase for the advertised ASINs.
Compare the median days-to-purchase to the current view-through window. If the median falls outside the window, a portion of real conversions is not being attributed to display.
Compare the median to the click-through window. A click-through window shorter than the typical consideration period misses late-converting clicks.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records the active click-through and view-through windows per line item and notes whether any documentation exists for the choice. For each line item, compare the window length to the product category's typical purchase cycle. A line item with windows the team can justify against a known purchase cycle passes. A line item where the team cannot explain why the window is set to its current value, or where all line items carry the same default regardless of category, is a flag recorded with the line item name and the active window values.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the active windows and any available rationale for each line item above to complete the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}}'s Amazon DSP line items are running attribution windows that have not been reviewed against the product purchase cycle. Default windows may overcount or undercount conversions depending on how long the category's shoppers typically take to decide, and window length effectively defines the platform's claim that an impression influenced a conversion. {{voice_recommend}} reviewing the typical days-to-purchase for the advertised products, then setting the view-through window to a length where a causal connection to the impression is plausible, and documenting the rationale so future changes have a baseline to compare against.

audit_checks.title: View-through window is long enough to claim credit for conversions display did not influence
audit_checks.impact_rationale: An inflated view-through window attributes purchases to display that would have happened regardless, overstating display ROAS and distorting the budget allocation signal. Campaigns that appear to perform well because of window length rather than actual influence receive more budget at the expense of channels that genuinely drove the outcome.

educate.base:
View-through attribution (VTA) counts a conversion when a shopper was served an impression, did not click, and then converted within the view-through window. The logic is that the impression raised awareness or reinforced intent even without a click. The window length determines how far back that claim extends.

A 30-day VTA window on a product a shopper buys monthly attributes the next subscription delivery or routine reorder to the display campaign. A 14-day VTA window on a fast-moving consumable with a 10-day repurchase cycle does the same thing for the majority of conversions. In each case, the conversion was going to happen regardless of the impression but the window marks it as display-influenced.

The practical effect shows up in reported ROAS. A campaign with a long VTA window against a short-cycle category reports a higher ROAS than a campaign with a tighter window, not because the ads work better, but because the window covers more of the organic purchase rhythm. When that inflated ROAS feeds budget decisions, spend shifts toward display on the basis of a number that flatters the channel.

A few signs suggest the VTA window may be too long:
  • The view-through window equals or exceeds the average repurchase cycle for the advertised product
  • View-through conversions represent a large share of all reported conversions, particularly for awareness campaigns with broad reach
  • The business does not see revenue lift that matches what the reported display ROAS implies
You may want to treat the view-through window as a hypothesis about causal reach. If you would not argue to a skeptical stakeholder that an impression from 28 days ago drove a conversion today, the window is longer than the claim supports.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For endemic advertisers, Amazon Marketing Cloud (AMC) can model attribution under different window assumptions, letting you compare reported conversions under a 7-day versus a 14-day versus a 30-day VTA window against the same underlying data. Running that comparison before committing to a window gives a more grounded picture than accepting the default.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For non-endemic advertisers using the Amazon Ad Tag pixel, view-through conversions are tracked against off-Amazon actions. The claim that a display impression drove a website lead or a form fill within 14 or 30 days is harder to substantiate, and you may want to apply a shorter window or rely more heavily on click-through attribution, where the connection to the ad is explicit.

learn_more.source_title: Amazon Marketing Cloud: Discover advertising insights
Amazon DSP: Advertise with a demand-side platform

investigate.intro: Compare the active view-through window to the product's typical repurchase or conversion cycle, and examine what share of reported conversions are view-through.

amazon_dsp_console.label: Amazon DSP console
steps: In the DSP console, open a campaign's reporting and break conversions into click-through and view-through columns. Note what percentage of total reported conversions are view-through across display and video line items.
Open the line item settings and confirm the active view-through window.

Compare that window to the product's typical repurchase cycle. For subscription or consumable products, the repurchase interval is often available from the account team or from the brand's internal data.

amazon_marketing_cloud.label: Amazon Marketing Cloud (AMC)
steps: In AMC, run an attribution window sensitivity analysis: compute conversions under a 7-day, 14-day, and 30-day VTA window for the same line items over the same date range.

If conversions rise sharply as the window lengthens past the product's typical repurchase cycle, the longer windows are capturing organic purchases rather than display-influenced ones. Note that delta in the audit.

Note the ratio of click-through to view-through conversions. A large view-through share on a broad awareness campaign is not unexpected, but the share should be explainable by the campaign's reach and the product's consideration cycle.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records the view-through window and the view-through share of total conversions per line item. For each line item, compare the window to the product's repurchase or consideration cycle. A view-through window meaningfully shorter than the repurchase cycle, combined with a view-through conversion share the team can explain, passes. A view-through window that equals or exceeds the repurchase cycle, or a view-through conversion share that appears high relative to the campaign's reach, is a flag recorded with the line item name, the window value, and the view-through conversion share.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the view-through window and the view-through conversion share for each line item above to complete the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}}'s Amazon DSP campaigns are running a view-through attribution window that may be capturing conversions the display impressions did not meaningfully influence. When the window extends past the typical repurchase or consideration cycle for the advertised products, conversions that were going to happen regardless get assigned to display, which overstates the channel's ROAS. {{voice_recommend}} comparing the active view-through window to the product's repurchase interval, running an attribution window sensitivity analysis in Amazon Marketing Cloud (AMC) if access is available, and tightening the window to a length where the causal claim is defensible. Documenting the rationale alongside the window setting makes future reviews faster.

audit_checks.title: Last-touch attribution model has not been evaluated for multi-format campaigns
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Last-touch attribution, Amazon DSP's default, assigns all credit to the most recent ad interaction before the conversion. For campaigns spanning prospecting display, retargeting, and streaming TV, last-touch systematically undervalues upper-funnel exposure and overvalues the retargeting impression that closed a sale the earlier touchpoints opened. Budget decisions made from last-touch data penalize awareness investment without evidence that awareness does not contribute.

educate.base:
Amazon DSP defaults to last-touch attribution: the ad interaction closest in time to the conversion receives full credit. For single-channel campaigns or short funnels, last-touch is easy to read and act on. For multi-channel or multi-format campaigns that include prospecting display, retargeting, and streaming TV (STV) or online video (OLV), last-touch produces a skewed picture.

Consider a shopper who sees a prospecting display ad on Tuesday, a streaming TV ad on Thursday, and a retargeting display ad on Saturday, then purchases on Sunday. Last-touch assigns the full conversion to the retargeting line item. The prospecting and streaming TV exposures, which may have built the awareness that made retargeting effective, receive no credit. Reporting then shows retargeting performing well and prospecting and STV performing poorly. Budget shifts toward retargeting, which continues until the top-of-funnel audience shrinks because prospecting investment fell.

Amazon Marketing Cloud (AMC) is the primary tool for moving past last-touch on Amazon DSP. AMC's path-to-conversion queries expose the full sequence of ad exposures before each conversion, and AMC supports custom attribution models, including time-decay and data-driven approaches, that distribute credit across the path. This analysis requires AMC access and sufficient conversion volume to produce statistically meaningful paths.

A few signs suggest the attribution model has not been evaluated:
  • The account reports by last-touch only and no AMC path analysis has been run
  • Budget decisions shift away from prospecting and STV toward retargeting based on last-touch numbers alone
  • The account team cannot describe which model is active or why it was chosen
You may want to treat the attribution model as a measurement design decision rather than a default to accept, particularly when the campaign spans more than one format or funnel stage.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For endemic advertisers, AMC's path-to-purchase queries can show the conversion lift associated with the full ad sequence versus retargeting alone. Running that query against a defined conversion event, such as a first purchase of a specific Amazon Standard Identification Number (ASIN), gives a concrete basis for evaluating how much the prospecting exposures contributed.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For non-endemic advertisers using the Amazon Ad Tag pixel, last-touch attribution on display is particularly prone to retargeting credit inflation because the retargeting pool is built from site visitors who were already interested before seeing the Amazon ad. You may want to consider whether the retargeting audience would have converted without the Amazon retargeting exposure, and run an incrementality test or holdout study if the budget justifies it.

learn_more.source_title: Amazon Marketing Cloud: Discover advertising insights

investigate.intro: Confirm which attribution model is active, whether any AMC path analysis has been run, and whether budget decisions are being made from last-touch data alone.

amazon_dsp_console.label: Amazon DSP console
steps: In the DSP console, confirm the attribution model applied to the account's reporting. Note whether any non-default model is active or whether reporting is on last-touch.

Review the performance breakdown by campaign type: prospecting, retargeting, STV, and OLV. If retargeting line items show substantially higher ROAS than prospecting and upper-funnel formats, note this as a potential last-touch inflation signal worth investigating.

amazon_marketing_cloud.label: Amazon Marketing Cloud (AMC)
steps: If AMC access is available, check whether any path-to-conversion or attribution model analysis has been run in the past 90 days.

If no analysis exists, note this as a gap. Running a basic path query that shows how many conversions involved a prospecting or STV touchpoint before the final retargeting click gives a starting point for evaluating whether last-touch understates upper-funnel contribution.

If AMC is not available for the account, note that the account is limited to last-touch reporting and that assessing multi-touch contribution requires either AMC access or a third-party measurement solution.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records the active attribution model and whether any AMC path-to-conversion analysis has been run. An account running a deliberate non-last-touch model, or one that has run AMC path analysis and made a documented decision to use last-touch based on what the data shows, passes. An account running last-touch with no path analysis and no documented rationale, particularly one allocating budget based on that last-touch data across multiple campaign formats, is a flag recorded with a note on whether AMC access is available and whether conversion volume is sufficient for path analysis.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the active attribution model and whether any AMC path analysis is available above to complete the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}}'s Amazon DSP reporting relies on last-touch attribution with no documented evaluation of whether that model fits the campaign structure. Last-touch assigns all credit to the final ad interaction before a conversion, which understates the contribution of prospecting display and streaming TV when the campaign spans multiple funnel stages. {{voice_recommend}} running a path-to-conversion analysis in Amazon Marketing Cloud (AMC) to surface how many conversions involved an upper-funnel touchpoint before the retargeting close, then deciding deliberately whether to stay on last-touch or apply a time-decay or data-driven model. The decision and its rationale should be documented so future budget reviews are grounded in the same measurement logic.
